title: AI Is the New MLM — And We’re All Participating
author: Nishi Mayekar
text:
AI Is the New MLM — And We’re All Participating
How machine learning became the most successful marketing story of the decade
Artificial intelligence is everywhere.
It writes our emails.
It drafts our resumes.
It generates art.
It summarizes meetings.
It optimizes workflows.
We’re told it will reshape civilization.
But what if much of what we’re witnessing isn’t a technological awakening — but a marketing phenomenon?
What if AI, at least in its current form, resembles something structurally similar to a multi-level marketing system?
Not illegal. Not fake.
But amplified by hype, incentives, and collective participation.
It Feels Like Magic. It Isn’t.
Modern systems like ChatGPT, developed by OpenAI, are built on large language models (LLMs). These systems are trained on enormous datasets containing licensed material, human-created training data, and publicly available text.
At their core, they do one thing:
They predict the next most likely token (word or word fragment) based on context.
They do not:
- Possess awareness
- Understand meaning in a human sense
- Think or reason independently
Instead, they operate through:
- Neural networks with billions of parameters
- High-dimensional vector math
- Probability distributions
- Optimization via gradient descent
The transformer architecture that powers these models (introduced in 2017) allowed systems to better capture long-range relationships in text. That technical leap made conversational AI dramatically more coherent.
It feels mystical because the interface is conversational. But underneath, it is advanced statistical pattern prediction at scale. Extremely sophisticated and its still a math.
If you think we had something similar running before..
Before generative AI :
- We had predictive text.
- We had automation software.
- We had recommendation systems.
- We had grammar tools like Grammarly.
- We had chatbots.
What changed or should I say got an update?
- Compute became more powerful and accessible.
- Cloud infrastructure matured.
- Venture capital flooded the sector.
- The user experience became seamless.
AI didn’t suddenly invent automation. It unified and scaled it.
And it wrapped it in a compelling narrative.
The MLM Parallel
The comparison to multi-level marketing isn’t about fraud. It’s about structure.
Here’s what we’re seeing:
- Massive hype cycles.
- Influencers saying, “Learn AI or be irrelevant.”
- Thousands of AI tools launching weekly.
- Startups building wrappers around existing AI APIs.
- Courses teaching people how to build AI products using other AI products.
Early adopters build products on top of foundational models. They monetize access. The real money often sits at the infrastructure layer — model providers, cloud platforms, chip manufacturers.
Meanwhile, the average user pays subscriptions.
It’s not a scam. But it is an incentive-driven amplification cycle.
Corporate Adoption: Innovation or Cost Efficiency?
Most companies are not using AI to reinvent humanity.
They are using it to:
- Automate repetitive tasks
- Draft internal documents
- Analyze large datasets
- Reduce operational costs
- Increase speed
All of this was technically possible before but it simply wasn’t cost-effective at scale.
AI APIs made it cheap enough. So now adoption looks like transformation, when in many cases it’s optimization.
If subscription costs rise or ROI plateaus, some of these integrations may quietly disappear. Not because AI failed — but because economics always win.
The Data Question — And Our Complicity
Large AI models are trained on enormous mixtures of licensed datasets, human-created training data, and publicly available web content.
That includes:
- Articles
- Forums
- Code repositories
- Creative writing
- Public social platforms
This has sparked legal and ethical debates. Some creators argue their work was used without meaningful consent. Companies argue they operate within legal frameworks and offer opt-outs or licensing agreements.
The reality is complicated.
But here’s the uncomfortable part:
We are participants. Many of us — including me — use free AI tools daily. To write better. To learn faster. To compensate for skill gaps. To move quicker.
These systems are expensive to train and run. The free access phase helps companies:
- Expand adoption
- Collect usage feedback
- Improve model performance
- Build dependency before monetization scales
It’s easy to criticize “big tech extracting data.” It’s harder to admit we benefit from the outputs.
We question the ethics — while actively using the tools. The system persists because it provides value.
The Environmental Cost
Training and running large AI models requires enormous computational power.
That means:
- Massive data centers
- High-performance GPUs
- Significant energy usage
- Water used for cooling systems
Some companies are investing in renewable energy and efficiency improvements. But demand is increasing rapidly. As AI becomes embedded in everyday workflows, its environmental footprint grows.
This isn’t a moral panic. It’s an economic and infrastructural reality.
Scale has consequences.
So Is AI a Scam?
No.
Machine learning works. The mathematics is real. The utility is undeniable. But the framing often exceeds the function.
AI today is:
- Large-scale pattern recognition
- Accelerated synthesis
- Cheaper automation
- A productivity amplifier
It is not consciousness.
It is not general intelligence.
It is not magic.
The skepticism isn’t about capability.
It’s about narrative inflation.
The Final Irony
This article was refined with the help of AI.
Because it’s easy.
Because it’s quick.
Because it removes friction.
Because it’s accessible.
Because it improves clarity.
Because it rewards convenience over effort.
And that may be the most honest conclusion of all:
AI isn’t a scam. But the story around it might be the most successful marketing campaign of the decade.
And whether we’re skeptical or enthusiastic —
We’re all participating.
